scene.description: Engine 181 is positioned on a rural road for wildland structure protection. A single hose line is stretched from the engine toward a firefighter protecting the exposure side of a house from advancing brush fire.  All labels are generated from this JSON using overlay coordinates.

problems.title: Engine 181 Wildland Structure Protection
problems.question: Engine 181 is protecting a structure from advancing brush fire. The crew stretched 200′ of 2″ hose to a wildland structure protection nozzle flowing 100 gpm at 100 psi. There is no elevation change and no appliance loss. What pump pressure should you set?
formulaBreakdown: PP = 16 FL + 100 NP + 0 elevation + 0 appliance loss
problems.instructorExplanation: Use the 2″ hose C value of 8. Calculate friction loss for the 200′ stretch, then add the 100 psi wildland nozzle pressure.
problems.explainMistake: Common mistakes are using a 1¾″ C value instead of the assigned 2″ C value, forgetting that 200′ equals 2 hundred-foot sections, using 50 psi nozzle pressure instead of the listed 100 psi wildland nozzle pressure, adding appliance loss when there is no appliance, or trying to flow 100 gpm through 1″ hose without recognizing the extreme friction loss.

problems.title: Engine 181 Wildland Structure Protection
problems.question: Engine 181 is protecting a structure from advancing brush fire. The crew stretched 250′ of 2″ hose to a wildland structure protection nozzle flowing 100 gpm at 100 psi. There is no elevation change and no appliance loss. What pump pressure should you set?
formulaBreakdown: PP = 20 FL + 100 NP + 0 elevation + 0 appliance loss
problems.instructorExplanation: Only the hose length changed. The longer 2″ stretch increases friction loss from 16 psi to 20 psi.
problems.explainMistake: Common mistakes are using a 1¾″ C value instead of the assigned 2″ C value, forgetting that 200′ equals 2 hundred-foot sections, using 50 psi nozzle pressure instead of the listed 100 psi wildland nozzle pressure, adding appliance loss when there is no appliance, or trying to flow 100 gpm through 1″ hose without recognizing the extreme friction loss.

problems.title: Engine 181 Wildland Structure Protection
problems.question: Engine 181 is protecting a threatened structure, but the line is changed to 200′ of 1″ forestry hose while still trying to flow 100 gpm at 100 psi. Use C value 150 for the 1″ hose. There is no elevation change and no appliance loss. What pump pressure would the math require?
formulaBreakdown: PP = 300 FL + 100 NP + 0 elevation + 0 appliance loss
Operational note: this is not a practical setup for that flow
problems.instructorExplanation: Only the hose size changed, but the C value jumped from 8 to 150. Trying to move 100 gpm through 200′ of 1″ hose creates extreme friction loss. The correct tactical fix is to lower the flow, shorten the stretch, or use larger hose.
problems.explainMistake: Common mistakes are using a 1¾″ C value instead of the assigned 2″ C value, forgetting that 200′ equals 2 hundred-foot sections, using 50 psi nozzle pressure instead of the listed 100 psi wildland nozzle pressure, adding appliance loss when there is no appliance, or trying to flow 100 gpm through 1″ hose without recognizing the extreme friction loss.

problems.title: Engine 181 Wildland Structure Protection
problems.question: Engine 181 is protecting a threatened structure with 200′ of 1″ forestry hose and a low-flow forestry nozzle flowing 30 gpm at 100 psi. Use C value 150 for the 1″ hose. There is no elevation change and no appliance loss. What pump pressure should you set?
formulaBreakdown: PP = 27 FL + 100 NP + 0 elevation + 0 appliance loss
problems.instructorExplanation: This is a practical low-flow 1″ forestry hose setup. The lower flow keeps friction loss manageable, but it is not the same fire stream as the 100 gpm 2″ structure protection line.
problems.explainMistake: Common mistakes are using a 1¾″ C value instead of the assigned 2″ C value, forgetting that 200′ equals 2 hundred-foot sections, using 50 psi nozzle pressure instead of the listed 100 psi wildland nozzle pressure, adding appliance loss when there is no appliance, or trying to flow 100 gpm through 1″ hose without recognizing the extreme friction loss.

nozzles.name: Low-flow forestry nozzle
nozzles.use: Mop-up, patrol, and limited reach operations
